acceptanceRules: PRIME RULE: any content that touches the private governance content plan must be approved against that source of truth. Verify with the user for manual approval in this case.
A flagged word is not a defect by itself. Do not rewrite solely to remove a review word.
Do not fix by swapping one flagged word for a synonym. That usually keeps the same weak sentence with different decoration.
Default to ACCEPTED when a flagged word is literal, concrete, domain-normal, and tied to the product or technical subject.
Words such as quality are expected in code-metric contexts; comprehensive is acceptable when it literally describes breadth, such as a broad library or documentation set.
Check with the parent governance content plan if changes are needed there, which require executive approval. Always verify with the user for manual approval in this case.
A review phrase is acceptable when it is concrete, accurate, product-specific, and the best word for the job.

If the sentence is generic, hype-like, or could appear on another developer-tool website unchanged, fix the substance: add the AtlasArc feature, metric, workflow, or consequence that makes the claim true.
If the sentence adds no information, consider deleting it instead of polishing it. Verify with the user for manual approval in this case.

Em-dash findings are punctuation/style findings and are generally forbidden. Prefer sentence splits, colons, semicolons, or commas; do not merely swap punctuation if the sentence still has generic contrast-rhythm.

findings.exactText: High class count means the package is structurally large. That may be appropriate for a domain model, a comprehensive utility library, or generated code. It may also mean the package was never decomposed and is holding multiple responsibilities by accident. Compare with fan-in: a large package with high fan-in is a shared foundation many others depend on; a large package with low fan-in may have grown without a clear design reason.
findings.reason: Review phrase: "comprehensive". Check whether the usage is concrete, intentional, and product-specific.
findings.needs: Do not rewrite solely to remove this word, and do not synonym-swap. ACCEPT if the sentence is concrete, literal, and product-specific; otherwise add the actual AtlasArc feature, workflow, metric, or consequence, or delete the sentence.
findings.fingerprint: deterministicphrase:comprehensivesrc/content/metrics/class-count.md14High class count means the package is structurally large. That may be appropriate for a domain model, a comprehensive utility library, or generated code. It may also mean the package was never decomposed and is holding multiple responsibilities by accident. Compare with fan-in: a large package with high fan-in is a shared foundation many others depend on; a large package with low fan-in may have grown without a clear design reason.

findings.exactText: Class count is a size metric, not a quality metric. A highly focused, well-abstracted package with thirty types is better than a package with one class that has three hundred methods. Use class count to orient yourself and to identify where decomposition conversations are worth having, not to grade package quality directly.
findings.reason: Review for context only. Common words such as quality are acceptable when literal and tied to a concrete feature, metric, workflow, or screenshot.

Use class count to orient yourself and to identify where decomposition conversations are worth having, not to grade package quality directly.

findings.exactText: Coverage metrics require a JaCoCo report to be loaded in AtlasArc. If no report is loaded, coverage fields show no value. The metrics reflect the specific test run that produced the report. Coverage also does not measure test quality: a package can have 100 percent line coverage from a test that asserts nothing. Treat coverage as a risk indicator for untested paths, not a badge of test quality.
findings.reason: Review for context only. Common words such as quality are acceptable when literal and tied to a concrete feature, metric, workflow, or screenshot.

If no report is loaded, coverage fields show no value. The metrics reflect the specific test run that produced the report. Coverage also does not measure test quality: a package can have 100 percent line coverage from a test that asserts nothing. Treat coverage as a risk indicator for untested paths, not a badge of test quality.

findings.exactText: High LOC means the package or class is large by source volume. In some packages that is expected: a comprehensive library, a generated file, or a large domain model. In most packages, high LOC combined with high fan-in signals that the package has become a load-bearing wall. Too much depends on it and it has grown to match. Classes with individually high LOC are usually the more actionable finding.
findings.reason: Review phrase: "comprehensive". Check whether the usage is concrete, intentional, and product-specific.
findings.needs: Do not rewrite solely to remove this word, and do not synonym-swap. ACCEPT if the sentence is concrete, literal, and product-specific; otherwise add the actual AtlasArc feature, workflow, metric, or consequence, or delete the sentence.
findings.fingerprint: deterministicphrase:comprehensivesrc/content/metrics/lines-of-code.md14High LOC means the package or class is large by source volume. In some packages that is expected: a comprehensive library, a generated file, or a large domain model. In most packages, high LOC combined with high fan-in signals that the package has become a load-bearing wall. Too much depends on it and it has grown to match. Classes with individually high LOC are usually the more actionable finding.

findings.exactText: LOC is the crudest metric in the set and should rarely be used alone. It does not distinguish between dense, sophisticated code and sprawling, repetitive code. It does not account for generated files or test code mixed in with production code. Use LOC as a proxy for mass: where to look and roughly how much is there, not as a proxy for quality or complexity.
findings.reason: Review for context only. Common words such as quality are acceptable when literal and tied to a concrete feature, metric, workflow, or screenshot.

findings.exactText: Confidence signals mark where a metric could be read the wrong way. Drill-down panels carry a "Keep in mind" note for when a metric misleads, and small packages show a low-confidence badge where a ratio metric would otherwise look alarming. The metrics reference explains the caveats.
findings.reason: Review for context only. Common words such as quality are acceptable when literal and tied to a concrete feature, metric, workflow, or screenshot.
